articles.title: Parts of Telangana brace for heatwave spell - The New Indian Express
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Telangana
articles.full_text:
HYDERABAD: Telangana is likely to witness heatwave conditions in some northern districts over the next three days, with maximum temperatures expected to rise gradually by 2-3 degrees Celsius, according to the Hyderabad Meteorological Centre.
The weather department issued a heatwave warning for a few northern districts even as isolated rainfall activity is expected in parts of the state.
SW monsoon likely to advance further
According to the latest weather analysis recorded at 8.30 am on Sunday, conditions are becoming favourable for the southwest monsoon to advance further over the next two to three days into additional parts of the southeast Arabian Sea, southwest and southeast Bay of Bengal, remaining parts of the Andaman Sea and Andaman & Nicobar Islands, as well as some areas of the east-central Bay of Bengal.
Meteorologists said a trough or wind discontinuity has formed from a cyclonic circulation over West Vidarbha, extending across Marathwada, interior Karnataka and Tamil Nadu up to the Gulf of Mannar at a height of 0.9 km above mean sea level.
Meanwhile, the low-pressure area over the southwest Bay of Bengal weakened on Saturday evening, reducing the possibility of widespread rainfall across Telangana.
Officials said the rise in temperatures, combined with dry weather, could intensify heat stress in northern districts.
Anganwadi teacher dies of ‘sunstroke’
Bhupalpally: A 35-year-old woman, Ch Latha, died of suspected sunstroke in Budharam village on Sunday. Officials said Latha had gone to distribute nutritional supplements to pregnant women in the village on Saturday. She reportedly fell ill due to the severe heat while returning home. Family found her unconscious at home and rushed her to a hospital for treatment. However, she died while undergoing treatment. A case has been registered.

articles.title: 44°C Heatwave in Hyderabad: Escape the Scorching Sun at These Refreshing Weekend Getaways - NativePlanet
articles.full_text:
Hyderabad is currently sweltering under an intense heatwave. The India Meteorological Department (IMD) issued an orange alert until May 20. Temperatures are expected to touch a brutal 44 degrees Celsius this week. Local residents are now seeking urgent relief from this scorching weather in nearby hill stations and lakes. This surge in heat makes city life very uncomfortable for many people.
This heatwave poses serious health risks for those venturing outdoors. Staying hydrated is essential for everyone during these peak hours. Many families are choosing weekend road trips to higher elevations. These destinations offer a much-needed break from the city's stifling heat and rising pollution levels. Exploring these cooler spots provides a refreshing escape from the burning sun and concrete heat.
Cooling Escapes from Hyderabad Heatwave Alert
Ananthagiri Hills is the closest escape at just eighty kilometers away. It sits at a higher elevation than the main city. This creates a cooler microclimate that is perfect for day trips. Many visitors enjoy the dense forests and peaceful trekking trails during the early mornings. The fresh forest air helps drop the temperature by several degrees compared to Hyderabad.
Laknavaram Lake provides a scenic retreat with its calm waters. Nearby Pocharam Wildlife Sanctuary offers cool breezes near the reservoir. For a colder experience, Lambasingi remains a popular choice despite the longer drive. Known as the Andhra Kashmir, it is ideal for an overnight stay away from the plains. These water bodies and hills provide a natural shield against the summer.
Travel Tips for Hyderabad Heatwave Alert
Transport options remain available for last-minute travelers. The Telangana State Road Transport Corporation (TSRTC) operates frequent bus services to these spots. Booking a private cab or using the South Central Railway (SCR) are also great options. These services help you reach your destination without facing the direct sun. Always check for seat availability before you plan your departure from home.
Planning ahead ensures a smooth trip during this warning period. Check for hotel availability and carry plenty of drinking water. These nearby retreats offer the best chance to recover from the intense sun. Stay safe while exploring these beautiful and refreshing local gems this summer. A quick trip can make a huge difference for your health and comfort during this week.

articles.title: Hyderabad Heatwave Intensifies as Residents Seek Cooler Getaways - urbanacres.in
articles.full_text:
Hyderabad is entering one of its harshest pre-monsoon phases in recent years, with temperatures forecast to reach 44°C and the India Meteorological Department maintaining an orange alert through May 20. The prolonged heat spell has reinforced concerns about the city’s growing climate vulnerability, as dense urban development, shrinking green cover and rising concrete footprints make metropolitan areas more susceptible to extreme temperature spikes. For many residents, the intense heat has turned routine outdoor activities into health risks, prompting a surge in short-distance travel to cooler locations within a few hours of the city.
The current conditions highlight how rapidly rising temperatures are reshaping mobility patterns across Hyderabad. With heat stress escalating, families and working professionals are increasingly seeking higher-altitude or water-rich destinations for weekend recovery. Nearby hill clusters and reservoir-based recreation zones are witnessing a clear uptick in demand, reflecting how climate extremes are influencing not just public health but also leisure behaviour and local tourism circuits. These microclimate-driven getaways offer temporary relief from the heat trapped within the city’s densely built-up zones. Ananthagiri Hills, situated roughly 80 kilometres away, has emerged as one of the most accessible escape points. Its forested terrain and elevation offer naturally cooler air, drawing visitors looking for shaded trails and early-morning treks. Locations such as Laknavaram Lake and the surrounding eco-spots near Pocharam Wildlife Sanctuary are also gaining traction, where breezes over the water surface help moderate the temperature compared to Hyderabad’s heat-absorbing pavements and rooftops. For those willing to travel farther, Lambasingi continues to attract attention as a significantly colder alternative, though its distance makes it more suited to overnight stays.
The popularity of these destinations also underscores systemic gaps in urban heat adaptation within Hyderabad. While the Telangana State Road Transport Corporation and the South Central Railway offer reasonable connectivity to many of these areas, residents are increasingly relying on such trips due to the limited availability of shaded public spaces, cooling centres or heat-relief infrastructure within the city itself. As temperatures rise each year, mobility choices are becoming part of personal resilience strategies, especially for families seeking safer environments during severe heat spells.
Officials advise travellers to plan ahead, secure accommodation early and carry adequate drinking water, particularly during extended journeys outside the city. With day temperatures expected to spike further this week, residents are prioritising destinations that provide both respite and cleaner air, reinforcing a broader shift toward climate-responsive travel patterns across the region. The ongoing heatwave serves as another reminder that urban centres must accelerate investments in heat-mitigation planning—expanding tree cover, improving public shade and redesigning neighbourhoods to withstand extreme climate events—so that safety and comfort do not rely solely on leaving the city during the hottest weeks.
